heroOverview: Microstomia (ICD-10: Q18.5) is a congenital condition characterised by an abnormally small oral opening, resulting from excessive or premature fusion of the maxillary and mandibular facial processes during embryonic weeks 4 to 7. The narrow mouth limits oral intake in infancy, impairs speech articulation, restricts dental and airway access, and can create progressive functional problems as the child grows. Microstomia may be an isolated anomaly or a feature of syndromes including Freeman-Sheldon syndrome (whistling face syndrome), Hallermann-Streiff syndrome, restrictive dermopathy, and trisomy 18. Reported prevalence of isolated congenital microstomia is below 1 in 100,000 live births, though exact incidence is poorly characterised because borderline cases are often not registered. Management requires a multidisciplinary craniofacial team, and treatment ranges from surgical commissurotomy and commissuroplasty to long-term oral stretching therapy and dental management.

keyStats.prevalence: Below 1 in 100,000 live births for isolated congenital microstomia; more common in the context of syndromic diagnoses
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally; no ethnic predominance identified in available literature
keyStats.avgAge: Detected at birth or on prenatal ultrasound; surgery typically performed in infancy or early childhood depending on severity and syndrome
keyStats.globalCases: Very rare in isolation; case literature numbers in the hundreds globally

definition: Microstomia (ICD-10: Q18.5) is defined as an oral aperture that is smaller than 2 standard deviations below the mean for age and sex on standardised anthropometric norms. It is classified under congenital malformations of face and neck (ICD-10 block Q10–Q18). The oral fissure is normally approximately 35–45 mm at term birth and grows proportionally with the face; in microstomia, the horizontal distance between commissures is reduced, restricting the oral aperture. The condition arises from excessive, premature, or asymmetric fusion of the facial processes, or from soft-tissue contracture secondary to connective tissue or skin disorders. Syndromic associations include Freeman-Sheldon syndrome (AD, caused by MYH3 variants), Hallermann-Streiff syndrome, restrictive dermopathy (LMNA or ZMPSTE24 variants), epidermolysis bullosa, ectodermal dysplasias, and chromosomal trisomies. Acquired microstomia — from burns, systemic sclerosis (scleroderma), oral submucous fibrosis, or craniofacial radiation — shares the clinical features of its congenital counterpart and is managed similarly. The functional impact includes restricted feeding and nutrition in infancy, difficulty with oral hygiene and dentistry, limited airway access for intubation (a significant anaesthetic challenge), impaired speech, and psychosocial distress. Management is coordinated across craniofacial surgery, speech-language pathology, dentistry, anaesthesiology, and genetics.

typesClassification.description: An abnormally small oral opening without other major anomalies; represents the rarest and simplest form, generally with excellent surgical outcomes.
Microstomia occurring as a feature of a broader genetic syndrome such as Freeman-Sheldon syndrome or trisomy 18; requires comprehensive multidisciplinary assessment and staged treatment.

typesClassification.type: Microstomia secondary to skin or connective tissue disease
typesClassification.description: Progressive restriction of the oral aperture caused by a skin disorder (e.g. epidermolysis bullosa, restrictive dermopathy) or systemic connective tissue disease (e.g. scleroderma) affecting oral commissure mobility.
Post-burn contracture, post-radiation fibrosis, or scarring from chronic infections progressively narrows the mouth opening; managed with release procedures and commissuroplasty.

primarySymptoms: A visibly small oral aperture at rest, narrower than expected for the infant's or child's age, often striking on first inspection.
Difficulty latching during breastfeeding due to the inability to open the mouth wide enough to form a proper seal around the nipple.
Reduced oral intake in neonates and infants, with inadequate weight gain if untreated.
Difficulty inserting a standard-sized spoon, dental instrument, or laryngoscope blade into the mouth.
Speech articulation difficulty, particularly with sounds requiring wide oral opening or precise tongue-to-lip contact.
Drooling in some patients due to impaired voluntary mouth control and lip seal mechanics.
Restricted dental hygiene — inability to brush the posterior teeth adequately, contributing to caries and periodontal disease.
Difficulty chewing and eating solid foods, particularly large pieces that require wide opening.
Airway management challenges — difficult mask ventilation and intubation represent a significant anaesthetic concern noted by all involved clinicians.
Psychosocial distress related to facial appearance, social interaction limitations, and self-consciousness about eating in public.
earlyWarningSigns: Neonatal feeding difficulty disproportionate to maternal milk supply — infant unable to latch or tires rapidly during feeds.
Weight loss exceeding 10% of birth weight in the first week without another explanation.
Visible tightness at the commissures when the infant attempts to cry with maximal mouth opening.
Prenatal ultrasound showing an abnormally small fetal oral aperture or a typical facial profile for Freeman-Sheldon syndrome (pursed lips, H-shaped chin crease).
emergencySigns: Neonatal airway obstruction requiring emergency intubation — the small oral opening may preclude standard laryngoscopy and require fibreoptic or surgical airway.
Severe failure to thrive with dehydration and electrolyte imbalance requiring intravenous nutrition.
Respiratory compromise in a neonate with coexisting micrognathia and posterior tongue displacement (Robin sequence).
Acute feeding aspiration with respiratory distress from a failed attempt to feed orally without medical supervision.

causes.cause: Premature or excessive facial process fusion
causes.description: During embryonic weeks 4–7, the maxillary and mandibular facial prominences normally fuse at the commissure to create the appropriate oral width. Premature or overly extensive fusion reduces this width, producing the small aperture characteristic of congenital microstomia.

causes.cause: Pathogenic variants in myosin heavy chain (MYH3)
causes.description: Freeman-Sheldon syndrome, the best-characterised syndromic cause of microstomia, is caused by dominant gain-of-function variants in MYH3, encoding embryonic skeletal myosin heavy chain. The resulting facial muscle stiffness constrains oral opening from early fetal life.

Loss-of-function variants in genes encoding lamin A or its processing enzyme cause restrictive dermopathy, in which abnormally rigid skin globally constrains fetal movement and facial expansion, resulting in tight facial skin, small mouth, and joint contractures.

Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome) and trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) produce characteristic facial features including a small oral aperture; the mechanism involves dosage imbalance of transcription factors regulating neural crest cell–derived facial development.

Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) causes progressive fibrosis of the perioral tissues, reducing the oral aperture over years; oral submucous fibrosis from areca nut (betel quid) use produces a similar fibrotic restriction of the buccal mucosa and commissures.

Thermal burns or therapeutic radiation to the perioral area produce collagen scar that contracts the commissures medially, progressively reducing the oral aperture over months; this is the most common cause of acquired microstomia globally.

riskFactors.description: MYH3 gain-of-function variants causing Freeman-Sheldon syndrome are autosomal dominant with high penetrance; an affected parent confers a 50% per-pregnancy recurrence risk.
Prenatal or postnatal diagnosis of trisomy 18, trisomy 13, or chromosome 4p deletion substantially raises the probability of associated facial restrictions including microstomia.

riskFactors.factor: Connective tissue disorder in mother or child
riskFactors.description: Inherited disorders of collagen or lamin (Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, restrictive dermopathy) that affect skin and fascial compliance can restrict facial growth and oral aperture.

riskFactors.factor: Burns or radiation to the perioral region
riskFactors.description: Thermal burns and head-and-neck radiation therapy are the leading causes of acquired microstomia; preventive mouth-opening exercises and prosthetic stents initiated immediately after injury can slow contracture formation.

Habitual chewing of areca nut is the primary cause of oral submucous fibrosis in South and Southeast Asia, and cessation of areca nut use is the single most effective prevention and treatment strategy.
Some autosomal recessive conditions associated with microstomia have higher frequency in offspring of consanguineous parents; genetic counselling should address this risk.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants with congenital syndromic diagnoses, identified at birth
Children with Freeman-Sheldon syndrome — affecting both sexes equally, globally distributed
Adults with systemic sclerosis, predominantly women aged 30–55
Adults in South and Southeast Asia with oral submucous fibrosis from areca nut use
Burns survivors of any age with perioral involvement
diagnosisOverview: Microstomia is identified clinically by measuring the interlabial horizontal distance at rest and at maximal voluntary opening and comparing this against normative charts for age and sex. A maximum opening below 35 mm in adults or below 2 standard deviations for age in children is the commonly used clinical threshold. For congenital cases, a full dysmorphology examination is essential to determine whether the condition is isolated or syndromic. The facial profile in Freeman-Sheldon syndrome — deep-set eyes, pursed lips, H-shaped chin crease, and joint contractures — is characteristic and should prompt MYH3 gene testing. Chromosomal microarray is indicated when additional dysmorphic features or organ anomalies are present. In acquired cases, the history of burns, radiation, or connective tissue disease usually clarifies the cause. Functional assessment includes a feeding evaluation by a speech-language pathologist, dental examination for caries and hygiene deficits, and a pre-anaesthetic airway assessment. Radiological evaluation with panoramic dental X-ray or CT identifies mandibular and temporomandibular joint involvement relevant to surgical planning. Prenatal diagnosis by ultrasound is possible in severe syndromic cases where the facial profile is distinctive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the horizontal oral aperture at rest and at maximum opening against age-specific norms, defining severity and tracking treatment progress.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Calipers or a millimetre ruler measures the mouth width — painless and takes under a minute.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects chromosomal imbalances underlying syndromic microstomia, guiding prognosis and counselling for associated organ anomalies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample from the patient; results in 2–4 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms Freeman-Sheldon syndrome in children with the characteristic facial and musculoskeletal phenotype, enabling accurate recurrence-risk counselling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a molecular genetics laboratory; results in 4–8 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates swallowing mechanics, oral motor function, and aspiration risk, guiding the route of nutrition before and after surgical intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Observation of a full feed, video-fluoroscopic swallow study if aspiration is suspected; non-invasive, takes 30–60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the degree of intubation difficulty to allow advance planning of fibreoptic bronchoscopy, videolaryngoscopy, or surgical airway before any procedure requiring general anaesthesia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinical assessment by an anaesthetist who measures mouth opening, visualises the oropharynx, and reviews prior anaesthetic records.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses dentition, alveolar bone, and temporomandibular joints, which are frequently affected in syndromic microstomia and in acquired cases with fibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single panoramic X-ray taken outside the mouth; takes a few minutes and uses minimal radiation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of microstomia is tailored to its cause and severity. For congenital isolated microstomia, surgical commissurotomy — releasing the narrow commissure and reconstructing the oral aperture to appropriate width — is performed in infancy or early childhood, using commissuroplasty techniques similar to those used in macrostomia repair in reverse. In syndromic cases, surgery is staged according to the broader treatment plan and the child's growth and development. Oral stretching therapy using graded commissure stretchers or stacked tongue depressors is an important non-surgical adjunct, used both pre- and postoperatively to maintain and increase oral opening. For acquired microstomia from burns or scleroderma, commissurotomy or mucosal advancement flaps are combined with splinting and physiotherapy. In oral submucous fibrosis, cessation of areca nut use is paramount; surgical biopsy-guided release, intralesional steroids, and physiotherapy are used to manage fibrotic bands. Pre-anaesthetic airway planning is required before any intervention under general anaesthesia. Dental management addresses hygiene, caries, and orthodontic needs throughout childhood and adolescence. Speech therapy begins after surgical repair and targets the acquisition of sounds impaired by the restricted aperture.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral stretching therapy with commissure stretchers
medicalTreatments.description: Graded acrylic or silicone stents placed at the commissures and progressively widened over months are used to maintain and slowly increase the oral aperture, particularly after surgical release and in scleroderma-related microstomia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series report improvements of 5–15 mm in maximum oral aperture over 3–6 months of consistent use; most effective when started early before fibrosis is established.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional corticosteroid injections (oral submucous fibrosis)
medicalTreatments.description: Triamcinolone acetonide or dexamethasone injected directly into fibrotic bands in the buccal mucosa reduces fibroblast activity and partially restores tissue elasticity in oral submucous fibrosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of 50–70% for symptom improvement reported in controlled trials; most effective in early-stage disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech and language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Post-surgical therapy targets sounds affected by limited oral opening, develops compensatory strategies during the stretching phase, and supports social communication in school-aged children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Most children with isolated microstomia achieve functional communication with speech therapy following surgical repair; outcomes are more variable in syndromic cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Nutritional support
medicalTreatments.description: Nasogastric or gastrostomy tube feeding is used in neonates and infants with severe microstomia who cannot achieve adequate oral intake; this bridges the gap until surgical correction and oral feeding rehabilitation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Prevents malnutrition and dehydration in 100% of cases managed appropriately; weaning to oral feeding is achievable in most after commissurotomy.

surgicalOptions.name: Commissurotomy with commissuroplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical release of the restricted commissure with mucosal and skin advancement to create a wider, naturally shaped oral aperture; the orbicularis oris muscle is reconfigured to maintain the new position.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves planned oral aperture width in approximately 85–90% of congenital cases; revision surgery is required in roughly 10–15% due to scar contracture.

surgicalOptions.name: Mucosal advancement flap and Z-plasty
surgicalOptions.description: For acquired microstomia, Z-plasty or mucosal advancement techniques release scar bands and prevent linear re-contracture at the commissure; combined with post-surgical stenting for best results.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good to excellent outcomes in 80–90% of patients with burn-related acquired microstomia when combined with post-surgical stretching physiotherapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and jaw exercises
alternativeTreatments.description: In acquired microstomia, structured manual stretching and therabite device exercises can slow contracture progression and maintain oral aperture when performed consistently from the time of initial injury.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pentoxifylline (oral submucous fibrosis)
alternativeTreatments.description: Pentoxifylline, a methylxanthine that reduces fibroblast proliferation, has been used in early oral submucous fibrosis with modest evidence for slowing progression; it is used adjunctively with cessation of areca nut use.

whySeeSpecialist: Microstomia requires a craniofacial plastic surgeon experienced in commissure reconstruction for surgical planning and execution. A clinical geneticist or dysmorphologist should evaluate any child with additional features. Because intubation risk is real and potentially life-threatening, a specialist anaesthetist must assess the airway before any planned procedure. Speech-language pathologists and paediatric dietitians support feeding and communication, and a paediatric dentist manages oral hygiene that cannot be achieved through a restricted aperture.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a craniofacial or paediatric plastic surgeon with experience in perioral reconstruction and ideally with access to a multidisciplinary craniofacial team. Confirm that the centre has paediatric anaesthetists skilled in difficult airway management, as this is essential for safe surgery. For scleroderma-related microstomia in adults, a rheumatologist managing the systemic disease should coordinate care with a maxillofacial or plastic surgeon.
preventionStrategies: Genetic counselling and prenatal testing for families with a known MYH3 variant or chromosomal syndrome that includes microstomia.
Cessation of areca nut (betel quid) chewing to prevent oral submucous fibrosis, the leading preventable cause of acquired microstomia in endemic regions.
Immediate post-burn oral stretching exercises and commissure stenting to prevent contracture formation after perioral thermal injury.
Early monitoring and oral exercises in patients receiving head-and-neck radiation therapy to limit radiation-induced fibrosis.
Routine newborn examination, including measurement of oral aperture in any infant with feeding difficulty, to identify microstomia early.
lifestyleModifications: Use narrow-based specialist bottle teats and small, shallow spoons appropriate for the restricted oral opening before surgical repair.
Perform oral stretching exercises consistently as prescribed by the speech-language pathologist or physiotherapist.
Maintain meticulous oral hygiene with a small-headed toothbrush and interdental aids appropriate for the oral aperture.
Attend regular dental check-ups at least every 6 months, as caries risk is elevated due to restricted hygiene access.
For acquired microstomia, wear the prescribed oral stent or splint as directed, even during uncomfortable periods, to maintain surgical gains.

recommended: High-calorie, nutrient-dense foods in small portions that fit comfortably into the restricted oral opening
Soft, minced, and puréed foods that require minimal chewing and can be introduced through a small aperture
Adequate protein and micronutrients (zinc, vitamin C) to support wound healing after surgery
Oral nutritional supplements if caloric intake from food alone is insufficient
avoid: Hard, large-bite foods that require wide mouth opening, particularly in the first months after commissurotomy
Areca nut, betel leaf, and tobacco-containing chewing products in patients with or at risk of oral submucous fibrosis
Acidic, spicy, or rough-textured foods that irritate healing mucosal wounds after surgical release

exerciseGuidelines: Physiotherapy-directed oral and perioral stretching exercises are a cornerstone of management. The Therabite device and stacked tongue depressor protocols provide measurable, progressive stretching and should be performed 3–5 times daily as instructed. General physical activity is not restricted. After commissurotomy, avoid activities that stretch the mouth widely for 4–6 weeks; thereafter, progressive oral exercises accelerate recovery.
dailyManagement: Perform prescribed oral stretching exercises at the recommended frequency — consistency is more important than force.
Use a small-headed or paediatric electric toothbrush to reach posterior teeth; consider a water flosser as an adjunct.
Track oral aperture measurement monthly with a ruler and report any decrease in opening to the surgical team promptly.
Ensure the child wears any prescribed oral stent during the specified hours each day.
Attend all multidisciplinary follow-up appointments, including dental, surgical, and speech therapy reviews.
prognosis: The prognosis for congenital isolated microstomia treated promptly with commissurotomy and post-surgical stretching therapy is good; most children achieve a functional oral aperture, satisfactory cosmesis, and age-appropriate speech. Syndromic microstomia carries a prognosis determined mainly by associated anomalies — particularly cardiac defects, joint contractures, and intellectual development — rather than by the oral defect itself. Acquired microstomia from burns or scleroderma tends to be progressive and recurrent despite surgery, requiring repeated stretching and, in some cases, multiple surgical interventions over years. Oral submucous fibrosis progresses to irreversible fibrosis and carries a low but definite risk of oral malignancy unless areca nut use is permanently ceased.
recoveryTimeline: After commissurotomy in infants, swelling peaks at 24–48 hours and subsides by 2 weeks. Oral feeding from a modified teat resumes within 48 hours in most cases. Full wound healing occurs by 4–6 weeks, at which point progressive oral stretching exercises begin. Functional feeding improvement is typically apparent within 2–4 weeks. Speech therapy begins 1–2 months after repair. Scar maturation and final commissure width are assessed at 6–12 months.
complications: Re-contracture of the commissure — the most common long-term complication, occurring in 15–30% of acquired cases and requiring repeat release.
Wound dehiscence after commissurotomy — uncommon in infants (under 10%) and managed by secondary healing or re-closure.
Persistent feeding or swallowing difficulty requiring prolonged nasogastric or gastrostomy tube use.
Difficult intubation and airway emergency during general anaesthesia — requires specialist planning before every surgical procedure.
Dental caries and periodontal disease from restricted oral hygiene access — requires intensive preventive dentistry.

Oral cancer (oral submucous fibrosis) — a rate of malignant transformation of approximately 7–13% over 10 years has been reported in advanced oral submucous fibrosis, predominantly in areca nut users.

supportResources.name: Freeman-Sheldon Syndrome (FSS) Research Group
supportResources.description: Patient and family organisation providing information, support, and connections to research trials for Freeman-Sheldon syndrome, the commonest syndromic cause of congenital microstomia.

supportResources.name: Children's Craniofacial Association
supportResources.description: US nonprofit providing educational resources and peer support for families of children with craniofacial conditions including oral aperture anomalies.

supportResources.name: Scleroderma & Raynaud's UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing guidance and support for adults with systemic sclerosis–related microstomia, including mouth care advice.

sources.title: Toyoda M et al. Freeman-Sheldon syndrome: report of three cases and literature review. Am J Med Genet, 1997
Kadish HA, Schunk JE. Microstomia in the emergency department. Pediatr Emerg Care, 2000
National Organization for Rare Disorders (NORD) — Freeman-Sheldon Syndrome
WHO International Classification of Diseases, 11th Revision (ICD-11): Congenital malformations of face and neck
Warnakulasuriya S et al. Nomenclature and classification of potentially malignant disorders of the oral mucosa. J Oral Pathol Med, 2007
Ware RE et al. Microstomia: an underrecognised feature of scleroderma. J Rheumatol, 1991
Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man (OMIM) — Freeman-Sheldon Syndrome (entry 193700)

confusedWithConditions.name: Macrostomia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Macrostomia is an abnormally wide oral opening from incomplete commissure fusion; microstomia is the opposite — an abnormally small opening from excessive fusion or contracture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ankyloglossia restricts tongue movement through a short lingual frenulum; it does not reduce the oral aperture size, whereas microstomia specifically narrows the interlabial opening.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other congenital lip malformations
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: General congenital lip anomalies encompass a variety of lip shape or volume defects; microstomia is specifically defined by reduced horizontal oral aperture width.

relatedConditions.name: Macrostomia
relatedConditions.relevance: The converse congenital oral commissure anomaly — excessive oral width — managed by the same craniofacial surgical team using related techniques.

relatedConditions.name: Other congenital malformations of lips
relatedConditions.relevance: Broader category of lip development anomalies that share the same embryological and clinical context as microstomia.

relatedConditions.name: Other congenital malformations of mouth
relatedConditions.relevance: Congenital oral anomalies co-occurring with microstomia in various syndromes, managed by overlapping specialists.

relatedConditions.name: Mandibulofacial dysostosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A common syndromic context for oral aperture restriction — jaw and facial bone hypoplasia compound the functional impact of reduced mouth opening.

faqs.question: What is microstomia?
faqs.answer: Microstomia is a condition in which the mouth opening is smaller than normal for a person's age. It can be congenital (present from birth) or acquired from burns, scarring, or conditions like scleroderma that cause soft-tissue contracture around the mouth.

faqs.question: What causes congenital microstomia?
faqs.answer: Congenital microstomia results from excessive or premature fusion of the facial processes during embryonic weeks 4–7, leaving a narrowed oral aperture. It can be isolated or part of a genetic syndrome such as Freeman-Sheldon syndrome or trisomy 18.

faqs.question: How is microstomia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical: the interlabial horizontal distance is measured and compared against age-specific norms. A measurement more than 2 standard deviations below the mean for age confirms microstomia. Additional tests assess whether it is isolated or syndromic.

faqs.question: Can microstomia be fixed surgically?
faqs.answer: Yes. Commissurotomy surgically widens the oral aperture to a planned normal size, combined with commissuroplasty to reshape the corner of the mouth. Success rates are approximately 85–90% for congenital cases; post-surgical stretching exercises maintain the result.

faqs.question: How does microstomia affect feeding in babies?
faqs.answer: A narrow oral opening prevents a proper latch for breastfeeding and makes bottle feeding difficult, causing spillage and inadequate intake. Specialised narrow-based teats and positioning help before surgery; commissurotomy typically resolves feeding problems in most infants.

faqs.question: What is Freeman-Sheldon syndrome?
faqs.answer: Freeman-Sheldon syndrome, also called whistling face syndrome, is a rare autosomal dominant condition caused by MYH3 gene variants. It features a small, pursed mouth (microstomia), deep-set eyes, H-shaped chin crease, and joint contractures. It is the best-characterised syndrome causing congenital microstomia.

faqs.question: Does microstomia affect speech?
faqs.answer: Yes. A restricted oral aperture limits sounds that require wide mouth opening or precise lip positioning. Most children achieve age-appropriate speech after commissurotomy and speech therapy, though outcomes in syndromic cases vary with associated features.

faqs.question: Is anaesthesia safe for children with microstomia?
faqs.answer: Standard intubation may be impossible through a very small oral opening, making anaesthesia in microstomia a specialist challenge. An experienced paediatric anaesthetist must plan the airway before any procedure, typically using fibreoptic bronchoscopy or video laryngoscopy.

faqs.question: What is oral submucous fibrosis?
faqs.answer: Oral submucous fibrosis is a progressive condition caused by habitual areca nut (betel quid) chewing, producing fibrotic bands in the cheeks and restricted mouth opening. It is the commonest cause of acquired microstomia in South Asia and carries a 7–13% long-term risk of oral cancer.

faqs.question: Can microstomia worsen over time?
faqs.answer: Congenital microstomia is stable unless scar contracture develops after surgery. Acquired microstomia from scleroderma or burns is often progressive without treatment. Oral submucous fibrosis worsens as long as areca nut use continues, making cessation essential.

faqs.question: What are commissure stretchers?
faqs.answer: Commissure stretchers are graded acrylic or silicone stents placed in the corners of the mouth and progressively widened over weeks or months to increase oral aperture. They are used after commissurotomy and in acquired microstomia to maintain surgical gains and slow re-contracture.

faqs.question: Will my child need hearing tests if they have congenital microstomia?
faqs.answer: Yes, if microstomia is syndromic. Branchial arch syndromes frequently include middle ear and ossicular anomalies causing conductive hearing loss. A newborn hearing screen and audiology follow-up are recommended for all children with congenital microstomia and additional features.

faqs.question: When is commissurotomy typically performed in infants?
faqs.answer: Surgical widening of the oral aperture is usually performed between 3 and 12 months of age for congenital cases, after the initial anaesthetic risk of the newborn period has decreased and before major speech development milestones.

faqs.question: How does scleroderma cause microstomia?
faqs.answer: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) causes progressive fibrosis of the skin and soft tissues throughout the body, including the perioral region. As the skin tightens, the oral aperture gradually narrows over years. Mouth exercises and specialist dental care can slow but not stop progression without systemic disease management.

faqs.question: Can microstomia prevent dental care?
faqs.answer: Yes. A small oral opening limits access for dental instruments, making routine cleaning, fillings, and extractions challenging. A paediatric or specialist dentist experienced with restricted apertures can use smaller instruments and adapt techniques; in some cases dental work requires general anaesthesia.

faqs.question: What specialists treat microstomia?
faqs.answer: Treatment is multidisciplinary: a paediatric craniofacial or plastic surgeon performs commissurotomy; a clinical geneticist evaluates for syndromes; a specialist anaesthetist manages airway risk; a speech-language pathologist and paediatric dentist provide ongoing functional care.

faqs.question: Is microstomia the same as small lips?
faqs.answer: No. Microstomia refers specifically to a narrow horizontal oral aperture — the distance between the corners of the mouth — not to lip volume or size. Thin or small lips (microcheilia) is a separate congenital condition involving lip tissue volume.

faqs.question: Can mouth exercises alone treat microstomia?
faqs.answer: For mild acquired microstomia or as an adjunct after surgery, mouth-opening exercises and commissure stretching devices can maintain and modestly increase aperture. They cannot correct significant congenital or established acquired microstomia on their own — surgery is required for meaningful improvement.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence risk for families?
faqs.answer: Isolated congenital microstomia is usually sporadic with low recurrence risk. Autosomal dominant syndromes such as Freeman-Sheldon carry a 50% per-pregnancy risk. A clinical geneticist calculates the specific recurrence risk after the diagnosis is confirmed.

faqs.question: How is dental hygiene managed with microstomia?
faqs.answer: A small-headed electric toothbrush, interdental brushes, and water flossers reach areas a standard brush cannot access through a narrow opening. Regular professional cleaning with specialist instruments is essential at least every 6 months, with fluoride varnish applications to reduce caries risk.

metaDescription: Microstomia is a congenital or acquired small mouth opening affecting feeding, speech, and dental access. Learn about commissurotomy surgery and management. Doctor-reviewed.
